skill.name: mtls-configuration
skill.description: Configure mutual TLS (mTLS) for zero-trust service-to-service communication. Use when implementing zero-trust networking, certificate management, or securing internal service communication.
skill.summary: Build and validate mTLS configuration for service mesh and zero-trust environments.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences, certificate path examples, URLs, and TLS terms as dangerous patterns. Review found no prompt injection, automatic command execution, credential exfiltration, or malicious network behavior. Residual risk is low because users could copy permissive or disabled mTLS examples into production without adapting them.

low_findings.title: Operational mTLS Weakening Examples
low_findings.description: The guide includes examples that set mTLS to PERMISSIVE, DISABLE a metrics port, use SIMPLE TLS for an external service, and skip Linkerd outbound ports. These are legitimate migration and compatibility examples, but they could reduce protection if copied into production without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines clearly show reduced or non-mutual TLS modes. The surrounding text presents them as examples, so the issue is misuse risk rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Broad Listener Binding in SPIRE Example
low_findings.description: The SPIRE server example binds to 0.0.0.0. This can be valid inside controlled Kubernetes networking, but it may expose the service more broadly if network policy and service controls are missing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bind address is explicit in the configuration. Exposure depends on deployment controls not shown in the guide, so confidence is high for the risk pattern but not for exploitability.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Execution and Secret Findings
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported Ruby backtick execution, certificate/key files, weak cryptography, SQLite usage, hardcoded URLs, and a critical code execution plus network plus credential heuristic. Review found these are Markdown examples, placeholder certificate paths, documentation links, and standard mTLS configuration terms, with no automatic execution or exfiltration evidence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is contained in Markdown diagrams, YAML examples, shell examples, or resource links. No evidence found of executable skill code, hidden instructions, credential collection, or outbound data transfer.

content.value_statement: Service teams need strong identity and encryption between internal workloads. This skill provides practical mTLS patterns for Istio, Linkerd, SPIFFE, cert-manager, and Kubernetes operations.
actual_capabilities: Explains mTLS flow, certificate hierarchy, and service mesh trust concepts.
Provides Istio PeerAuthentication and DestinationRule templates for strict and mutual TLS.
Shows cert-manager resources for issuing short-lived workload certificates.
Includes SPIFFE and SPIRE configuration examples for workload identity.
Lists command examples for checking certificate expiry and debugging TLS status.
Summarizes mTLS best practices for migration, rotation, monitoring, and verification.
limitations: Does not generate real certificates, private keys, or production secrets.
Examples require adaptation to each cluster, namespace, service mesh version, and policy model.
Does not replace a full PKI design, threat model, or compliance review.
Some examples intentionally use permissive or disabled modes for migration and debugging.

use_cases.title: Secure Kubernetes Services
use_cases.description: Create a baseline mTLS plan for internal service-to-service traffic in a Kubernetes environment.

use_cases.title: Migrate Legacy Workloads
use_cases.description: Plan a gradual move from permissive service mesh traffic to strict mTLS enforcement.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot TLS Failures
use_cases.description: Use targeted checks to inspect certificate expiry, authentication policy, destination rules, and proxy logs.

prompt_templates.title: Explain mTLS Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how mTLS works for two Kubernetes services using a sidecar service mesh. Include the certificate exchange and trust checks.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Istio Policies
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Istio mTLS configuration for namespace production. Use STRICT mode for payment-service and include verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a phased migration from permissive mTLS to strict mTLS for legacy and modern services. Include risks, observability checks, and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Multi-Cluster Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my multi-cluster mTLS design using SPIFFE, SPIRE, Istio, and cert-manager. Identify trust boundaries, rotation risks, and validation checks.

output_examples.input: How should I enable strict mTLS for one production service?
output_examples.output: The response identifies the service and namespace, recommends a workload-specific policy, explains the impact on callers, and lists verification commands.

output_examples.input: Why are my services failing after enabling mTLS?
output_examples.output: The response walks through certificate expiry, peer authentication, destination rules, proxy logs, and mesh-specific status checks.

output_examples.input: Can I use SPIFFE identities with my service mesh?
output_examples.output: The response explains trust domains, workload identifiers, SPIRE server and agent roles, and integration points with certificate issuance.

best_practices: Start with a migration plan, then move production workloads toward strict mTLS after validation.
Use short-lived workload certificates and monitor certificate expiry before enforcement changes.
Verify both client and server identity, trust chain, policy state, and proxy behavior after each change.
anti_patterns: Disabling mTLS in production only to bypass a handshake or routing issue.
Copying placeholder certificate paths, trust domains, hosts, or namespaces into production.
Treating mTLS as a complete authorization model without service identity and policy checks.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and command examples that users can adapt and run manually.

faq.question: Which service meshes are covered?
faq.answer: The guide covers Istio, Linkerd, SPIFFE, SPIRE, cert-manager, and Kubernetes-related certificate operations.

faq.question: Can I use the examples directly in production?
faq.answer: Treat them as starting points. Review namespaces, hosts, ports, trust domains, certificate paths, and mesh versions first.

faq.question: Does it create private keys or certificates?
faq.answer: No. It shows configuration patterns and placeholders, but it does not generate or store secrets.

faq.question: Can it help with compliance requirements?
faq.answer: It can support technical controls for encrypted service communication, but it does not replace a compliance assessment.

faq.question: What is the main security caution?
faq.answer: Avoid leaving permissive, disabled, or skip-port settings in production unless a documented exception requires them.
